coredata.dc:title: Box–Behnken design and machine learning optimization of PET fluorescent carbon quantum dots for removing fluoxetine and ciprofloxacin with molecular dynamics and docking studies as potential antidepressant and antibiotic
coredata.dc:creator: Ebere Enyoh, Christian
Wang, Qingyue
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the optimization, multifunctional applications, and ADMET (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, and Toxicity) evaluation of polyethylene terephthalate-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots (PET-FCQDs, size: 9.98 ± 10.90 nm) for removing fluoxetine (FLX; 100–400 ng/L) and ciprofloxacin (CIP; 50–150 µg/L) from aqueous solutions and exploring their therapeutic potential as antidepressants and antibiotics. Box-Behnken Design (BBD) and machine learning models—Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Support Vector Machines (SVM)—optimized removal efficiencies of 95.19 % for FLX and 97.85 % for CIP, with ANN achieving superior prediction accuracy (R2: 0.82–0.88). Adsorption adhered to Langmuir (FLX; R2 > 0.999) and Freundlich isotherms (FLX and CIP; R2 > 0.998) with intraparticle diffusion kinetics (R2 > 0.94). Maximum monolayer adsorption capacities were 705 ng/g for CIP and 62.27 ng/g for FLX. Mechanistic studies via ATR-FTIR and molecular dynamics revealed hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interactions as key drivers. Molecular docking highlighted PET-FCQDs’ intrinsic antidepressant potential (docking score: −8.7 kcal/mol for SERT) and their synergy with CIP (−7.3 kcal/mol for topoisomerase IV). ADMET analysis via SwissADME and ADMETSAR server confirmed high gastrointestinal absorption, positive Blood Brain Barrier, non-carcinogenicity, and non-biodegradability. However, high toxicity to ecological organisms such fish, honey bee and Tetrahymena Pyriformis raises concerns about potential ecological risks if released into environments This comprehensive study integrates experimental and computational approaches to optimize PET-FCQDs for environmental and pharmaceutical applications, showcasing their dual role as effective adsorbents for removing contaminants and as potential therapeutic agents. The findings provide a framework for the sustainable utilization of PET waste in developing multifunctional nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Box–Behnken design and machine learning optimization of PET fluorescent carbon quantum dots for removing fluoxetine and ciprofloxacin with molecular dynamics and docking studies as potential antidepressant and antibiotic

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Saitama University, 255 Shimo-Okubo, Sakura-ku, Saitama 338-8570, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Saitama University, 255 Shimo-Okubo, Sakura-ku, Saitama 338-8570, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the optimization, multifunctional applications, and ADMET (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, and Toxicity) evaluation of polyethylene terephthalate-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots (PET-FCQDs, size: 9.98 ± 10.90 nm) for removing fluoxetine (FLX; 100–400 ng/L) and ciprofloxacin (CIP; 50–150 µg/L) from aqueous solutions and exploring their therapeutic potential as antidepressants and antibiotics. Box-Behnken Design (BBD) and machine learning models—Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Support Vector Machines (SVM)—optimized removal efficiencies of 95.19 % for FLX and 97.85 % for CIP, with ANN achieving superior prediction accuracy (R2: 0.82–0.88). Adsorption adhered to Langmuir (FLX; R2 > 0.999) and Freundlich isotherms (FLX and CIP; R2 > 0.998) with intraparticle diffusion kinetics (R2 > 0.94). Maximum monolayer adsorption capacities were 705 ng/g for CIP and 62.27 ng/g for FLX. Mechanistic studies via ATR-FTIR and molecular dynamics revealed hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interactions as key drivers. Molecular docking highlighted PET-FCQDs’ intrinsic antidepressant potential (docking score: −8.7 kcal/mol for SERT) and their synergy with CIP (−7.3 kcal/mol for topoisomerase IV). ADMET analysis via SwissADME and ADMETSAR server confirmed high gastrointestinal absorption, positive Blood Brain Barrier, non-carcinogenicity, and non-biodegradability. However, high toxicity to ecological organisms such fish, honey bee and Tetrahymena Pyriformis raises concerns about potential ecological risks if released into environments This comprehensive study integrates experimental and computational approaches to optimize PET-FCQDs for environmental and pharmaceutical applications, showcasing their dual role as effective adsorbents for removing contaminants and as potential therapeutic agents. The findings provide a framework for the sustainable utilization of PET waste in developing multifunctional nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data are presented in the manuscript and supplementary materials

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of pharmaceutical contaminants in water systems has become an increasingly critical environmental concern due to their persistence, bioaccumulation potential, and adverse effects on ecosystems and human health[40]. Among these, fluoxetine (FLX), an antidepressant, and ciprofloxacin (CIP), a widely used antibiotic, are increasingly detected contaminants in wastewater and surface water at low concentrations in ng/L and µg/L (Weinberger et al., 2014; Yamindago et al., 2021; Timofeeva et al., 2022). Their extensive use and incomplete removal during conventional wastewater treatment processes have led to their widespread occurrence, posing significant ecological and public health risks[27,13]. Developing effective strategies for removing these pharmaceutical pollutants from aqueous environments has therefore become a pressing challenge.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), particularly those synthesized from waste materials have emerged as innovative and sustainable materials for environmental sensing and remediation as well as biomedical applications[24]. Plastic wastes have emerged as an attractive precursor for CQDs[2,1,8]. Polyethylene Terephthalate plastics (PET), a widely used plastic in packaging and textiles, is one of the most prevalent sources of plastic waste, contributing significantly to the global plastic pollution crisis[11]. Its persistence in the environment and challenges in recycling underscore the urgent need for effective upcycling strategies. The conversion of PET waste into fluorescent CQDs (PET-FCQDs) represents a promising approach to address both plastic pollution and water contamination simultaneously. These nanomaterials possess unique properties, including high surface area, tunable surface functionalities, photoluminescence, and excellent adsorption capacity[41,1,43], making them particularly suitable for removing pharmaceutical pollutants such as FLX and CIP.

The optimization of PET-FCQDs as functional adsorbents for pollutants like FLX and CIP is crucial for developing cost-effective and efficient systems to address low-concentration contaminants commonly present in natural and treated water. Traditional optimization methods, while effective, often require extensive experimental work. In this context, Response Surface Methodology (RSM) using Box-Behnken designs (BBD) offers a statistically robust framework for optimizing key operational parameters, such as pH, contact time, and pollutant concentration, while reducing experimental effort[21]. RSM facilitates the systematic exploration of interactions between these factors and predicts the optimal conditions for achieving maximum adsorption efficiency. The integration of machine learning (ML) further revolutionizes the optimization process by providing enhanced predictive capabilities and adaptive modeling for complex, nonlinear systems[17]. Algorithms such as Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Support Vector Machines (SVM) are particularly effective in analyzing large datasets and identifying intricate relationships between variables[17]. By complementing the traditional RSM framework, ML models refine predictions, improve optimization accuracy, and accommodate scalability for real-world applications. This dual approach ensures that the adsorption system is not only efficient but also adaptable for practical implementation in diverse environmental conditions.

There are no prior studies evaluating the adsorption of FLX and CIPs by CQDs and this study therefore aims to optimize the adsorption of FLX and CIP by PET-FCQDs in aqueous solutions using a combination of experimental and computational approaches. A BBD was employed to evaluate the effects of key variables such as pH, time, and concentration, while ML models, including ANN and SVM, were used to further refine the optimization process. Additionally, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were employed to unravel the molecular-level interactions and adsorption mechanisms of PET-FCQDs with FLX and CIP. This computational approach enables a deeper understanding of how these CQDs interact with contaminants at the atomic scale, providing critical information into adsorption dynamics, energy profiles, and surface chemistry.

Beyond their application in water treatment, CQDs are celebrated for their multifunctionality in biomedical studies, including their antibacterial, antifungal, and drug delivery properties (Chaudhary et al, 2021; [24]. Their small size, high surface area, unique electronic properties and potentially favorable ADMET (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity) profiles make them promising candidates for diverse therapeutic and diagnostic applications. In this study, molecular docking was also utilized to evaluate the potential of PET-derived CQDs as candidates for antidepressant and antibiotic activities. FLX, an antidepressant, and CIP, a broad-spectrum antibiotic, were modeled to explore the binding affinities and interactions of PET-CQDs with key biological targets. Furthermore, ADMET analysis were conducted to evaluate their pharmacokinetic properties. By integrating MD simulations and docking studies, this research broadens the scope of PET-CQDs, showcasing their potential as versatile materials for both environmental remediation and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemicals and preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Japan, provided the palladium, ciprofloxacin (CIP: C17H18FN3O3, analytical grade, 98 %), and fluoxetine (FLX: C17H18F3NO · HCl, analytical grade, 98 %). Without any additional purification, the chemicals were utilised just as they were sent by the manufacturer. All experiments were carried out using ultrapure water (Direct Q-3UV, Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany), unless otherwise noted.

Disposable water bottles made of commercial-grade PET were used as the source material to obtain the microplastics (MPs) required for CQD synthesis. The preparation of PET MPs has been thoroughly detailed in previous studies[18,21]. PET-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots (PET-FCQDs) were synthesized using a simple, one-step pyrolysis method (Fig. 1 ). In this process, 1 g of PET MPs was placed in a ceramic crucible and heated in a muffle furnace (Sibata Ceramic CM-150, Sibata Scientific Technology, Ltd., Japan) to 300 °C at a controlled heating rate of 10 °C/min. The temperature was maintained at 300 °C for 30 min. Following calcination, the resulting black powder was allowed to cool and then finely ground using a mortar and pestle. This powder, designated as PET-FCQDs, was collected and yielded an 85 % recovery. To prepare an aqueous solution for future applications, 1 g of the ground powder was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, resulting in a solution with a concentration of 0.1 g/mL. This straightforward synthesis method demonstrates the efficient conversion of PET waste into high-yield FCQDs suitable for further characterization and applications.

The characterization of PET-FCQDs was conducted using various analytical techniques to determine their optical, and structural properties: The absorbance and emission spectra (@excitation of 250 nm) of PET-FCQDs were measured using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer (UVmini-1240, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5300PC, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). These measurements provided insights into the photoluminescent behavior of the samples. The size and morphology of the PET-FCQDs (20 µL sample) were examined using a fluorescent microscope (MX6300, Meiji Techno Co., Japan) equipped with a 4 K Moticam4000 microscope camera. The analysis was conducted under visible light at a nanometer scale with a 100x magnification. The captured fluorescent microscope images were further processed and analyzed for size and shape using Motic Image Plus 2.3S software. Functional groups present on the surface of PET-FCQDs were identified using Attenuated Total Reflectance-Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy (JASCO FTIR-6100). Before measurement, the instrument was calibrated by blanking to ensure accuracy. The PET-FCQDs were securely positioned on the ATR crystal (diamond) for analysis. Infrared spectra were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm-1, averaging 64 scans at a resolution of 4 cm-1. This analysis provided molecular-level information about the surface composition, which is critical for understanding potential environmental degradation effects. The pH of point zero charge (pHpzc) was determined by pH drift method using KCl at pH 2 to 10 (adjusted using PBS buffer solutions).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design and data collection for PET-FCQDs-driven removal of FLX and CIP
ce:section.ce:para: To avoid the limitations of one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) analysis, the study employed a Box-Behnken Design (BBD) with three variables at two levels (−1 and + 1) using response surface methodology (RSM). The BBD approach evaluates responses within the experimental range and predicts the optimal conditions. The variables tested included initial concentrations of FLX (100–400 ng/L) and CIP (50–150 µg/L), pH (2–10), and contact time (5–240 min). These ranges were selected based on concentrations reported in the environment (Weinberger et al., 2014; Yamindago et al., 2021; Timofeeva et al., 2022). The BBD matrices for FLX and CIP are provided in Tables S1 and S2.

Batch adsorption experiments were conducted for each run under ambient conditions with a shaking speed of 100 rpm (Fig. 1b). Initial concentrations of FLX (100, 250, and 400 ng/L) and CIP (50, 100, and 150 µg/L) were prepared by serial dilutions from stock solutions (FLX: 1 mg/L; CIP: 1 g/L). These concentrations were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-mini 1240, Shimadzu, Japan) at wavelengths of 200–800 nm (Fig. S2), and calibration curves with an R2 value of 0.999 were generated (Fig. S2). For each experiment, 1.5 mL amber vials were filled with 200 µL of PET-FCQD solution (0.1 g/mL), followed by the addition of 300 µL of the selected sorbate concentration (FLX or CIP). The pH was adjusted using PBS buffer solutions and monitored with a pH meter (LAQUAtwin B-212, Horiba Scientific, Japan). At the end of each experiment, the solution was filtered through a 0.20 µm cellulose filter, and the filtrates were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The concentration of FLX and CIP in the solution was determined using UV absorbance, based on the Beer-Lambert law. The removal efficiency final removal concentration at a particular time (Ct) and adsorption capacity (qt) of FLX and CIP was calculated using equations (1)–(3).

The variables in the equation include: Co and Ct- initial and at specific time concentration of sorbate, Ao − the initial absorbance of sorbate (FLX/CIP), At − the absorbance of sorbate at a specific time, qt – adsorption capacity, v – volume (300 µL) and m- mass of PET-FCQDs (200 µL of concentration of 0.1 g/mL = 0.02 g).

The application of RSM, machine learning (ML) models, such as artificial neural networks (ANN), support vector machines (SVM), and kinetics make up the three main sections of the current study. Fig. 2 displays the flowchart for this investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RSM optimization process for FLX and CIP removal
ce:section.ce:para: The BBD based on the RSM was used in this work, and the Design Expert Software version 13.0 trial version (Stat-Ease Inc., Minneapolis, USA) was used for optimisation modelling. A statistically sound framework for optimising important operational parameters is provided by box-Behnken designs. In order to better understand the process, RSM may also be used to fit the answer to a final model equation based on linear, factorial, polynomial, or quartic models within the range under study. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) at p < 0.05 was used to determine the correctness of the RSM model, and desirability functions were used to assess the final numerical optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning driven optimization for FLX and CIP removal process
ce:section.ce:para: Machine learning optimization was implemented through SAS JMP Pro 16′s predictive modeling platform, which supports multiple programming languages including Python, JavaScript, C, SAS, and SQL. The experimental design investigated three key variables (concentration, time, and pH) at two levels across 13 and 15 runs for FLX and CIP treatment, respectively. The resulting design matrix informed the optimization process using ANN and SVM models. To ensure model reliability, the dataset was partitioned into training (70 %), validation (15 %), and testing (15 %) sets, with a learning rate of 0.1. Model consistency was maintained by setting a fixed random seed of 0, enabling direct comparison of different hyperparameters and algorithms. The models' robustness was evaluated through 5-fold cross-validation, with performance assessed via residual sum of squares (RSS), root mean square error (RMSE), and R-square (R2). All computations were performed on a system equipped with a 64-bit Intel Core i5 vPro processor and 4 GB RAM.

The machine learning approach employed Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) based Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) with backpropagation learning functionality. The network architecture consisted of three input parameters (concentration, pH, and time), one hidden layer with five neurons, and utilized hyperbolic tangent activation functions. The ANN system can be mathematically represented by equation (4), where f denotes the activation function, N represents the number of neuron inputs, and k indicates the layer type (hidden or output). The backpropagation algorithm optimized network weights either through batch processing of the entire dataset or sequential analysis of individual data points.

Support Vector Machine (SVM) analysis was implemented to identify the maximum margin hyperplane for predicting removal efficiencies. The model employed a radial basis function kernel (equation (5) to transform the dataset into higher dimensional space, with predetermined hyperparameters of cost and gamma both set to 10. The gamma parameter, selected prior to model training, controlled the decision boundary curvature, ultimately determining the model's predictive capabilities. where the kernel of the radial basis function is K(xi,xj). The Lagrange multiplier and threshold parameter are shown by αi and b, respectively. Similar to RSM, desirability functions were employed to determine the final optimisation of the ML models for removal efficiency.

The Desirability Function Approach optimizes multiple responses by converting individual response variables (yi, representing PET-FCQDs removal rates for each factor) into specific desirability functions (di) within defined ranges. This optimization process occurs in two phases: first, identifying independent variable levels that yield optimal dependent variable outcomes, and second, maximizing overall desirability based on controllable parameters (Mourabet et al, 2014).

To study the kinetics, two models have been examined: the Intraparticle diffusion (equation (12) and Dumwald-Wagner model (equation (13) while isotherm was studied by Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models (equations (15) and (16).

BIOVIA Material Studio 8 (Dassault Systèmes, USA) was employed for simulations. Based on ATR-FTIR analysis of PET-FCQDs and insights from relevant literature[1], the PET-FCQDs base structure was constructed in silico using 3D Atomistic modeling and subsequently optimized geometrically with the COMPASS Forcefield to minimize energy. The resulting PET-FCQDs structure consisted of 68 carbons, 5 hydrogens, and 9 oxygens (C68H5O9), totaling 82 atoms, with a net mass of 965.78 g and a Connolly surface area of 622.70 Å2 (Fig. S7). Structural data files for FLX and CIP were retrieved from PubChem and similarly optimized geometrically with the COMPASS Forcefield for energy minimization. Molecular adsorption and dynamics simulations were conducted using the “Adsorption Locator” and “Forcite” modules in Material Studio. For molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, a smart algorithm was utilized to minimize the simulation system, and dynamics were performed within the NVT ensemble at a system temperature of 298 K. The relaxation time was set to 0.1 ps. Non-bonded interactions, including van der Waals and electrostatic forces, were calculated using atom-based methods and Ewald summation with an accuracy of 0.001 kcal/mol. A cutoff radius of 0.95 nm was applied across all systems. The molecular dynamics simulation was run for 100 ps with a time step of 0.1 ps. The output of the before and after adsorption were analyzed including the temperature fluctuations, radial distribution function, X-ray scattering, dipole autocorrelation function and power spectrum. Furthermore, the following formula was ultimately used to calculate the interaction energies after the MD simulation of the FLX and CIP adsorption using the Forcite module:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking- antidepressant and antibiotic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The antidepressant and antibiotic potential of PET-FCQDs, both individually and in combination with known drugs, were assessed through molecular docking studies. The structures of PET-FCQDs, fluoxetine (FLX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and their adsorbed forms on PET-FCQDs were saved as protein data bank (PDB) files for use in docking simulations. FLX works by blocking or inhibition enzymes such as human serotonin transporter[3], while CIP inhibits DNA Gyrase (Topoisomerase II) as primary target, and Topoisomerase IV as secondary target[12]. Therefore, these enzymes were considered as target for the docking studies. Protein structures for these enzymes were obtained from the Protein Data Bank: SERT (PDB ID: 6AWP), DNA Gyrase (PDB ID: 4CKK), and Topoisomerase IV (PDB ID: 1ZVT) (Fig. S8a–c). These structures were prepared using Discovery Studio 4.5 software to ensure the reliability of the docking process. The software removed docked chemicals and crystallographic water molecules that might interfere with docking accuracy. Molecular docking was performed on the active sites of the receptor proteins using Autodock Vina within PyRx software version 0.8[22]. The resulting ligand-receptor complexes were visualized with Biovia Discovery Studio 4.5, highlighting hydrogen bonds and other hydrophobic interactions that contributed to the binding.

The pharmacokinetic features of the PET-FCQDs—absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity (ADMET) —was assessed using SwissADME and ADMETSAR server[19]. The Simplified Molecular Input Line Entry System (SMILES) for the reduced PET-CQDs structure (graphitic structure was reduced to 5 with same functional groups attached) was generated as O = C(O)c1cc2C(=O)Cc3cc(C(=O)O)c5CC(=O)Cc4c(O)c(c1)c2c3c45 and used for the ADMET analysis.

The characterization results of the as-synthesized PET-FCQDs is presented in Fig. 3 . Under normal daylight (Fig. 3a), PET-FCQDs appear as off-white to pale yellow or brownish particles without visible fluorescence, as confirmed in darkroom observations (Fig. 3ai-aii). However, under UV light (365 nm), they exhibit bright bluish fluorescence (Fig. 3aiii), indicating absorption of UV light and re-emission in the visible spectrum, a common feature of carbon-based nanomaterials. The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows strong absorbance at 270 nm and 341 nm due to π-π* transitions in the aromatic sp2 domains of PET-FCQDs [33], while n-π* transitions on C = O groups may occur at 314 nm[37,15]. Excitation at 250 nm produced a strong emission peak at 499 nm (Fig. 3c), corresponding to blue fluorescence, suggesting quantum confinement and surface state effects [49]. ATR-FTIR analysis (Fig. 3d) confirmed the presence of –CO, –COOH, and –OH groups on the PET-FCQDs' surface, while microscopy revealed irregular, non-spherical shapes with jagged edges (Fig. 3e). Particle size ranged from 2 to 50 nm, predominantly between 1–5 nm, with an average size of 9.98 ± 10.90 nm, offering a high surface area advantageous for adsorption and sensing applications. The pHpzc was determined to be 6.3 (Fig. 3f), indicating neutral surface charge at this pH. Below pH 6.3, protonation renders the surface positively charged, favoring adsorption of anionic species, while above pH 6.3, deprotonation results in a negatively charged surface, enhancing interaction with cationic species. This behavior makes pHpzc a critical parameter for optimizing adsorption processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal rates of FLX and CIP by PET-FCQDs from different experimental run
ce:section.ce:para: The removal rate or efficiencies (%) FLX and CIP by PET-FCQDs based on BBD is presented in Fig. 4 a while the experimental conditions for each run can be found in Table S1 and S2. For FLX, the highest removal efficiency (97.83 %) was observed at the highest concentration (400 ng/L, Run 1). This suggests that the PET-FCQDs may perform better at higher pollutant concentrations, possibly due to greater availability of FLX molecules for adsorption or degradation processes. However, in Run 10 (400 ng/L, pH 2, 122.5 min), the removal rate was 92.52 %, which is lower than the peak value, indicating that concentration alone is not the sole determinant of efficiency. Interactions with other factors like pH and time also play a role. The removal efficiency is higher at neutral to slightly acidic pH values (e.g., pH 6 in Run 1 with 97.83 % and pH 2 in Run 12 with 94.04 %). Lower efficiencies (e.g., 89.52 % in Run 6 at pH 6) suggest that highly acidic or alkaline conditions may not significantly improve the removal process. pH likely affects the charge of the FLX molecules and the surface charge of PET-FCQDs, influencing the interaction between the adsorbent and the pollutant. The removal rate at shorter times (e.g., 5 min in Run 1 with 97.83 %) is higher compared to longer durations (e.g., 240 min in Run 13 with 88.37 %). This indicates that the removal process may reach saturation quickly, with extended durations leading to diminishing returns. It is possible that most of the pollutant is removed rapidly in the initial phase, with minimal additional removal occurring over time.

For CIP, removal efficiencies are fairly consistent across concentrations (50 to 150 µg/L). However, higher concentrations (e.g., 150 µg/L in Run 3) show similar removal rates (96.21 %) to lower concentrations (e.g., 50 µg/L in Run 12 with 95.07 %). This implies that concentration alone does not drastically impact efficiency within the tested range. Acidic conditions (pH 2) generally result in higher removal rates, as seen in Run 15 (97.85 %). This is likely because acidic pH enhances the adsorption capacity or the degradation mechanism of PET-FCQDs. Previous studies on CIP adsorption by MPs have also shown removal of CIP at both low and high pH due to the nature of CIP[35–36,7]. Neutral pH (e.g., pH 6 in Run 1) also provides good removal efficiency (96.49 %), but the data suggests slightly reduced efficiency compared to acidic pH. Shorter times (e.g., 5 min in Run 15) show higher removal efficiency (97.85 %), while extended times (e.g., 240 min in Run 8) show a drop (93.86 %). This indicates that most of the removal occurs quickly, and prolonged exposure does not significantly enhance the process, potentially due to system saturation or degradation of the adsorbent.

Correlation analysis is important for evaluating data before model development because it helps identify the strength and direction of relationships between independent variables (e.g., concentration, pH, time) and the response variable (removal rate). By understanding these relationships, the most influential factors can be prioritized excluding variables with minimal impact, and detect potential interactions or non-linear effects that might require specific modeling approaches[17]. This preliminary step ensures the models, whether RSM or ML, are designed to capture the most relevant patterns, reducing complexity and improving predictive accuracy.

The correlation analysis between variables for FLX and CIP are presented in Fig. 4b and 4c. The correlation analysis for FLX shows that concentration has a moderate positive correlation (r = 0.76) with removal efficiency, making it the most influential variable (Fig. 4b). pH has a very weak positive correlation (r = 0.17), and time shows negligible correlation (r = -0.03), indicating that FLX removal is concentration-dependent and reaches equilibrium quickly, with minimal impact from extended reaction times or pH variations. For CIP, concentration has a weak positive correlation (r = 0.37), while pH (r = 0.09) and time (r = -0.31) have negligible or weak negative effects on removal efficiency (Fig. 4b). This suggests that CIP removal efficiency is less dependent on concentration and is slightly hindered by prolonged exposure due to equilibrium or desorption effects. The results suggest that for optimized model development using RSM and ML, FLX models should prioritize concentration as the primary factor, with pH and time included as secondary variables to capture interactions. CIP models should explore non-linear interactions, particularly between pH and time, given their subtle combined effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of reduced model equations and three-dimensional (3D) variable effect for the removal of FLX and CIP from an aqueous medium by PET-FCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal conditions for adsorbing FLX and CIP ions in aqueous solutions using PET-FCQDs were determined using linear and two-factor interaction (2FI) models, respectively, based on Box-Behnken Design (BBD) data. Model accuracy was assessed through analysis of variance (ANOVA), with results summarized in Tables S3-S4, including key metrics such as predicted values, adjusted R-squared values, means, and standard deviations. These metrics confirm the significance of the model fits for FLX and CIP removal rates under optimal conditions.

Following BBD optimization, updated linear and 2FI models were developed for FLX and CIP removal rates. These models demonstrated low coefficients of variation (2.12 for FLX and 0.52 for CIP) and significant Model F-values (4.62 for FLX and 9.37 for CIP), with p-values below 0.05, indicating minimal likelihood (0.29 %) of these results occurring due to noise. For FLX, concentration (A) was identified as the most significant model term, while for CIP, concentration (A), time (C), and the interaction between pH and time (B*C) were significant.

The ANOVA results also revealed coefficients of determination (R2) of 0.6078 for FLX and 0.8754 for CIP, confirming a strong correlation between predicted and experimental values. Additionally, adequacy of precision values—measures of the signal-to-noise ratio—were 5.69 for FLX and 11.78 for CIP, exceeding the threshold of 4, indicating that the models are robust for exploring the design space[21]. These findings demonstrate that the linear and 2FI models are well-suited for describing the removal rates of FLX and CIP by PET-FCQDs in aqueous solutions. The accuracy and reliability of the models are further illustrated in the graph comparing experimental and predicted values (Fig. 5 a-b).

The solution to the optimization of the PET-FCQDs driven adsorption of FLX and CIP from an aqueous medium show that, under room temperature followed established reduced linear and 2FI model equations (19)–(20);

To facilitate analysis of the reduced models and to illustrate the interactive effects of experimental variables on the PET-FCQD-driven adsorption process, 3D response surface plots were generated (Equations (19)–(20). Fig. 6 displays these surfaces, highlighting the percentage removal of FLX (Fig. 5c) and CIP (Fig. 5d-f). The height of the contour lines and the associated 3D shapes illustrate the interaction of key factors and their combined influence on adsorption efficiency. The surface colors convey the trends: dominant red regions indicate higher efficiency, while transitions to green, yellow, or blue signify a decline in removal performance.

The minimal influence of time and pH on FLX removal rates by PET-FCQDs, except at higher concentrations, reflects the robustness of the adsorption process across various pH and time conditions (Fig. 5c). Even though the removal rates were consistently high (∼95–96 %) across the pH range of 2–10, a closer analysis (Fig. S3) indicates slightly higher removal efficiencies at lower pH and shorter times (<10 min). These findings align with the relationship between the pHpzc of PET-FCQDs (6.3) and the pKa of FLX (9.8) [16]. FLX is a weak base with a pKa of 9.8, meaning it predominantly exists as a positively charged cation at pH < pKa and as a neutral molecule at pH > pKa. PET-FCQDs is positively charge when pH < pHpzc (6.3), which might create electrostatic repulsion with cationic FLX. Despite this, high removal rates suggest that other mechanisms, such as π-π stacking interactions or hydrogen bonding, dominate the adsorption process. At higher pH (>pHpzc), the PET-FCQDs surface becomes negatively charged, and the slightly positive and neutral FLX molecules interact with the adsorbent likely through electrostatic attraction, hydrophobic interactions or π-π interactions. Previous studies support these findings, indicating that FLX reaches equilibrium within shorter times (<20 min) and exhibits high removal rates at low pH when using Marine macroalga Bifurcaria bifurcata [44], biochar[23], and at high pH with PET microplastics[48]or carbon nanocomposites[38].

For CIP, distinct interaction patterns were observed between time and concentration (Fig. 5d), pH and concentration (Fig. 5e), and pH and time (Fig. 5f). The contour plot for time and concentration revealed that removal rates improved at shorter times (<100 min) and higher concentrations (50–150 µg/L). Previous studies involving plastic-based micro/nano adsorbent for CIP have reported similar findings (Lu et al, 2019; [50,21]. In most cases, higher initial CIP concentrations lead to greater removal rates, likely due to the saturation of adsorption sites on the PET-FCQDs. At higher concentrations, the stronger mass transfer driving force increases the adsorbent's capacity. The initial solute concentration acts as a driving force for adsorption, enhancing diffusion and mass transfer between the solution and the adsorbent surface[20]. However, the overall influence of initial concentration cannot be generalized, as various experimental conditions interact to impact the adsorption process for specific contaminants and adsorbents.

The interaction between pH and concentration reveals that while pH had an insignificant direct effect on CIP removal rates, increased concentrations consistently enhanced removal across the entire pH range of 2–10 (Fig. 5e). This suggests that the adsorption process is primarily driven by the availability of CIP molecules and the capacity of the PET-FCQDs rather than pH-dependent surface interactions. However, removal rates were notably higher at lower pH and shorter times (Fig. 5f), indicating that acidic conditions may play a supportive role in optimizing the removal rate. This behavior can be connected to the zwitterionic characteristics of CIP and the point zero charge (pHpzc) of PET-FCQDs (Fig. 3f). CIP exists as a cation, zwitterion, or anion depending on the solution pH [21]. Below its isoelectric point (approximately pH 6.1), CIP primarily exists as a positively charged cation. Similarly, the PET-FCQDs have a positively charged surface below their pHpzc (6.3). Despite potential electrostatic repulsion under these conditions, the high adsorption efficiency suggests that other forces, such as π-π interactions or hydrogen bonding, may dominate (see the MD, Fig. 7 d). At higher pH, where CIP transitions to an anionic form and the PET-FCQDs become negatively charged, electrostatic attraction between the adsorbent and adsorbate enhances removal rate. The observed trends indicate that while pH influences the charge states of both CIP and PET-FCQDs, concentration and other adsorption forces significantly drive the process, resulting in consistent removal rates across a wide pH range. This suggests the versatility of PET-FCQDs in removing CIP under varying environmental conditions.

In this study, we sought to maximize the removal efficiency of FLX and CIP from complex simulated wastewater through systematic optimization. Using the Design-Expert software's desirability function approach − a powerful tool for multi-response optimization − we identified the ideal operational parameters. This methodology, which has been widely employed in various fields from pharmaceutical processing to environmental remediation, allows for the simultaneous optimization of multiple response variables while considering practical constraints [25]. By analyzing the optimization desirability ramps (Fig. S4), we established distinct optimal conditions for each compound. For FLX removal, peak performance (95.19 % removal) was achieved at a concentration of 400 ng/L, pH 6, and a contact time of 5 min (Fig. 5g). Similarly, CIP showed excellent removal efficiency (97.85 %) under optimized conditions of 123.0 µg/L, pH 2, and a 5-minute contact time (Fig. 5h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning modeling development and optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The predictive capabilities of ANN and SVM models were evaluated for both FLX and CIP removal processes. The performance of the ML algorithm was evaluated using statistical metrics to ensure reliability, focusing on validation quality and prediction accuracy (Tables S1-S2). Fig. 6 summarizes the results, including RSS, RMSE, and R2 values. RSS measures the discrepancy between observed and predicted data, while RMSE quantifies the squared deviations between actual and predicted values from the test dataset. The R2 value, ranging from 0 to 1, reflects the correlation between predicted and experimental removal efficiencies, with 1 indicating perfect prediction and 0 showing no correlation.

For FLX removal, the ANN model demonstrated superior performance with R2 = 0.82 and r = 0.91, compared to SVM's relatively lower R2 = 0.62 and r = 0.79. The removal efficiency for FLX fluctuated between 88–98 %, with ANN showing higher RSS (17.41) but lower RMSE (1.26) compared to SVM (RSS = 28.11, RMSE = 1.59). In contrast, both models exhibited exceptional performance for CIP removal, with comparable R2 values (ANN: 0.88, SVM: 0.87) and strong correlation coefficients (ANN: r = 0.94, SVM: r = 0.93). CIP removal efficiency consistently ranged from 94-98 %, with both models showing excellent prediction accuracy as evidenced by their low RMSE values (ANN: 0.11, SVM: 0.09). The superior performance of both models for CIP compared to FLX might be attributed to the more consistent removal behavior of CIP under the tested conditions.

The machine learning (ML) optimization strategy aimed to maximize removal rates under predicted optimal conditions, paralleling the RSM approach. Using the desirability function profiler in JMP Pro 16, the ideal operational parameters for each compound were systematically identified (Fig. S5). The optimization process revealed distinct optimal conditions for FLX and CIP removal, with the ANN model predicting particularly promising results. For FLX, the ANN model predicted peak removal rate of 97.83 % under optimal conditions (400 ng/L concentration, pH 6, 5-minute contact time). CIP removal showed equally impressive results, with the ANN model predicting 97.79 % removal rate under its optimal conditions (67 µg/L concentration, pH 2.3, 5-minute contact time). Interestingly, the SVM model for CIP suggested different optimal parameters, predicting 96.78 % removal rate at 141.5 µg/L concentration, pH 10, and a notably longer contact time of 206 min. These findings demonstrate the potential variability in optimization strategies between different ML algorithms, with the ANN model generally favoring shorter contact times while maintaining high removal rates.

Comparing the optimized removal rate from RSM, ANN and SVM and experimentally determined values is presented in Fig. 6(c). The results showed a trend of experimental values < SVM < RSM < ANN for FLX and experimental values < SVM < ANN < RSM. This varying pattern between FLX and CIP could be attributed to the inherent differences in their removal mechanisms and the models' ability to capture the complexity of their respective removal processes.

Understanding the fundamental mechanisms and efficiency of adsorption processes requires comprehensive analysis of both kinetic and equilibrium aspects. Adsorption kinetics reveals the rate-controlling steps and mass transfer mechanisms, providing information into the temporal evolution of the adsorption process[32]. Meanwhile, adsorption isotherms characterize the equilibrium relationship between adsorbate concentration in solution and the amount adsorbed on the solid phase under constant temperature conditions[4]. Together, these theoretical frameworks enable the optimization of adsorption systems by elucidating the nature of adsorbate-adsorbent interactions, binding mechanisms, and surface properties. The calculated PET-FCQDs adsorption kinetics and isotherm data for FLX and CIP removal are presented in Table 1 while the linear regression plots are presented in Figs. S5 and S6. For adsorption kinetics, the intraparticle diffusion model showed strong fitting for both compounds (R2 > 0.94), with FLX having a higher boundary layer thickness (C = 1.35) compared to CIP (C = 0.72). This suggests a more significant surface diffusion effect for FLX. The Dumwald-Wagner model showed better fitting for CIP (R2 = 0.7476) compared to FLX (R2 = 0.6130), indicating that the pore diffusion mechanism might be more relevant for CIP adsorption.

For adsorption isotherms, both Langmuir and Freundlich models showed excellent fitting (R2 > 0.97), but the Langmuir for FLX (R2 = 0.999) and Freundlich for CIP (R2 = 0.999) models demonstrated slightly better correlation, which agrees with previous studies involving carbon-based adsorbents (Liu et al, 2019; [38]. This indicate heterogenous surface adsorption for CIP and monolayer adsorption for FLX. The maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (qmax) from the Langmuir model was significantly higher for CIP (705 ng/g) compared to FLX (62.27 ng/g), suggesting better adsorption efficiency for CIP. The Freundlich intensity parameter (n) values being close to 1 (0.945 for FLX and 0.999 for CIP) indicates favorable adsorption conditions for both compounds. The higher KL value for CIP (0.8478 L/µg) compared to FLX (0.000211 L/ng) in the Langmuir model suggests stronger binding affinity of CIP to the PET-FCQDs surface. The similar KF values between FLX and CIP (0.010 and 0.014 respectively) in the Freundlich model indicate comparable adsorption strength under heterogeneous surface conditions.

The molecular adsorption poses (side view) of FLX and CIP onto PET-FCQDs is presented in Fig. 7a and 7b while molecular simulation results provide information into the differences in adsorption dynamics and interactions between FLX and CIP with PET-FCQDs are summarized in Table 2 . The total energy for CIP (−79.55 ± 2.11 kcal/mol) is significantly lower than for FLX (3.04 ± 1.02 kcal/mol). This indicates that the adsorption process is thermodynamically more favorable for CIP compared to FLX. The adsorption energy for CIP (−38.30 ± 2.11 kcal/mol) is more negative than that for FLX (−24.23 ± 1.02 kcal/mol), suggesting stronger interactions between CIP and PET-FCQDs. This may be attributed to the zwitterionic nature of CIP, which allows it to interact with both positively and negatively charged groups on the PET-FCQD surface. The rigid adsorption energy for CIP (–32.13 ± 2.38 kcal/mol) is more negative than for FLX (−24.94 ± 1.22 kcal/mol), indicating a stronger initial interaction. However, CIP also shows a higher deformation energy (−6.17 ± 0.40 kcal/mol compared to 0.71 ± 0.75 kcal/mol for FLX), which reflects structural adjustments in the PET-FCQDs and CIP during adsorption. The binding energy for both FLX (163.97 ± 1.02 kcal/mol) and CIP (163.22 ± 1.11 kcal/mol) is comparable, indicating similar overall strength in binding affinity. However, this is varied by the differences in specific energy contributions (e.g., van der Waals vs. electrostatic). Van der Waals energies for both FLX (53.65 ± 3.47 kcal/mol) and CIP (55.42 ± 3.97 kcal/mol) exhibit similar contributions, indicating comparable hydrophobic interactions with the PET-FCQDs surface. CIP (−68.67 ± 4.12 kcal/mol) shows significantly stronger electrostatic interactions compared to FLX (−16.21 ± 4.68 kcal/mol). This reflects CIP's zwitterionic nature and its ability to interact more effectively with charged functional groups on PET-FCQDs. However, non-bond energy is much lower for CIP (−13.25 ± 4.96 kcal/mol) compared to FLX (37.44 ± 6.02 kcal/mol). This suggests that non-bonded interactions (e.g., hydrophobic and dispersion forces) are less critical for CIP compared to FLX, where they play a larger role in the adsorption process.

Generally, the results agreed with experimental findings and the stronger adsorption of CIP by PET-FCQDs is driven by more favorable electrostatic and rigid adsorption energies, likely facilitated by CIP's zwitterionic structure. In contrast, FLX interactions rely more heavily on non-bonded and van der Waals forces. These differences can also be related to the pKa values of FLX and CIP and the point zero charge (pHpzc) of PET-FCQDs. CIP's zwitterionic nature allows for efficient interaction across a broader pH range, while FLX, with a pKa of 9.8[16], may have limited interaction at neutral or acidic pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and dynamics analysis for FLX and CIP adsorption by PET-FCQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature equilibrium criteria serve as a key indicator for determining whether a model system has reached a balanced state. For equilibrium to be achieved, temperature fluctuations should stabilize within a range of 5 %–10 % [20]. In this study, the temperature variations observed during the adsorption dynamics (Fig. 7c) were within 10 % after 10 ps, indicating that the simulation system had successfully reached equilibrium. Achieving equilibrium is critical as it ensures the accuracy and reliability of the computational results[18].

Radial distribution functions (RDFs), also known as pair correlation functions, are fundamental tools in molecular dynamics simulations for analyzing atomic spatial distribution[22]. The RDF, denoted as g(r), quantifies the probability of finding a specific atom pair i, j separated by a radial distance r within a spherical shell of thickness dr. This relationship is mathematically described by the normalized RDF expression outlined in Equation (21) [34].

where N i,j(r) is the number of occurrences of atom pairs between r and r +dr

The values of RDFs analysis, g(r), obtained after evaluating the molecular dynamics simulation data for the different FLX/CIP-PET-FCQDs complex systems are displayed in Fig. 6(d). In the region below 3.5 Å, where chemical and hydrogen bonding interactions dominate (Enyoh et al, 2022), the pure PET-FCQDs exhibited distinct sharp peaks between 1–2 Å, indicating strong internal chemical bonding within the quantum dot structure. Upon drug loading, both PET-FCQDs + FLX and PET-FCQDs + CIP systems showed modified peak patterns in this region, suggesting the formation of new hydrogen bonds and chemical interactions between the drugs and the PET-FCQDs. These modifications in peak intensity and shape indicate direct chemical interaction between the adsorbates (involving surface extremely electronegative atom, such as F and O atoms) and the PET-FCQDs surface. In the region beyond 3.5 Å, where non-bonding interactions (electrostatic and van der Waals forces) predominate, all three systems displayed smaller, broader peaks with gradually diminishing intensity. The drug-loaded systems showed subtle variations in peak patterns compared to pure PET-FCQDs in this region, indicating the establishment of additional non-bonding interactions between the drugs and the PET-FCQDs surface. The overall RDF patterns suggest that both FLX and CIP interact with PET-FCQDs through a dual mechanism: strong chemical and hydrogen bonding at close range (<3.5 Å) and weaker non-bonding interactions at longer distances (>3.5 Å). This combination of interaction types likely contributes to the effective adsorption of both pharmaceutical compounds onto the PET-FCQDs surface, though with subtle differences in their local structural arrangements.

Small- and wide-angle X-ray scattering (SWAXS) has become a widely used technique for probing structural features in solution and identifying structural transitions induced by adsorption processes[29]. In this study, SWAXS measurements were employed to investigate the structural changes in PET-FCQDs before and after the adsorption of FLX and CIP. Using MD simulations, the scattered X-ray intensity (I) as a function of the scattering vector (2θ) was calculated, incorporating energy changes (ω) as described by Chen and Hub [9]. These calculations, presented in equation (22), highlight the differences in scattering intensity between the baseline PET-FCQDs simulation (A) and a second simulation containing adsorbed FLX or CIP molecules (B), revealing key insights into adsorption-induced structural modifications., i.e, I(2θ,t) = IA (2θ,t) – IB (2θ).

The SWAXS curves are presented in Fig. 6e. For PET-FCQDs, reflecting the intrinsic structure of the material with a gradual decrease in intensity as 2θ increases up till 15°, characteristic of nanoscale order. After FLX adsorption, there was a slight shift in intensity with reduction in 2θ (13°) compared to the PET-FCQDs. This suggests that FLX interacts primarily at the surface of the PET-FCQDs, inducing subtle structural changes with minimal impact on the internal structure of the PET-FCQDs. In contrast, after CIP adsorption, a more pronounced shifts in intensity and 2θ (11°) compared to the PET-FCQDs was observed. This indicates stronger structural responses, likely due to the zwitterionic nature of CIP, which promotes significant rearrangements on the PET-FCQDs surface. Comparing the curves, CIP shows a stronger effect on the scattering profile, likely forming more ordered adsorption complexes, whereas FLX induces relatively minor structural alterations. These results demonstrate the adaptability of PET-FCQDs to adsorb these pollutants while maintaining nanoscale structural integrity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dipole autocorrelation power spectrum and function
ce:section.ce:para:
The dipole autocorrelation function serves as a key tool for analyzing the infrared spectrum of adsorbed molecules. It is instrumental in understanding spectral line broadening and provides information into the dynamic interactions within the system[6]. The infrared spectra are calculated using the fast Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function of dipole moments[46,47,51]:

The dipole autocorrelation function power spectrum, as shown in the Fig. 6f, reveals the vibrational modes and dynamic interactions of PET-FCQDs before and after adsorption of FLX and CIP. The spectrum for bare PET-FCQDs displays a relatively sharp and distinct peak at low frequencies (< 5 cm−1), indicating the intrinsic vibrational characteristics of the material. Upon adsorption of FLX and CIP, the spectra exhibit peak shifts and formation of new peaks (> 10 cm−1). The broader spectral features for both PET-FCQDs + FLX and PET-FCQDs + CIP reflect additional vibrational modes introduced by the adsorbed molecules. These variations in intensity and peak broadening provide molecular-level evidence of successful adsorption and the distinct interaction dynamics of FLX and CIP with the PET-FCQDs. The shifts and variations in the power spectrum highlight the differences in the dipole moment dynamics, likely attributed to the molecular structure and functional groups of the adsorbed compounds[51]. However, the dipole autocorrelation function (DACF) plots for PET-FCQDs, PET-FCQDs + FLX, and PET-FCQDs + CIP (Fig. 6g) reveal differences in the temporal decay of dipole moment correlations, offering insights into molecular interactions and dynamics. For PET-FCQDs, the DACF shows a rapid decay over time, indicating relatively unrestricted dipole fluctuations and minimal intermolecular constraints due to the absence of adsorbed molecules. In the PET-FCQDs + FLX system, the DACF exhibits a slower decay, suggesting restricted dipole fluctuations caused by interactions between the PET-FCQDs and FLX molecules. The adsorption of FLX likely alters the local electronic environment, stabilizing dipole correlations over a longer time frame. In contrast, the PET-FCQDs + CIP system demonstrates an even slower DACF decay, indicating stronger or more sustained interactions between PET-FCQDs and CIP. This stronger interaction may result from the chemical structure of CIP, which promotes more effective bonding or adsorption on the PET-FCQD surface.

The adsorption mechanisms were also monitored by FTIR (Fig. 8 a) and the overall mechanism are presented in Fig. 8b-c. The mechanisms were evaluated first by adsorption kinetics and isotherm models and the mechanism indicate that the adsorption processes involve mainly both monolayer and heterogenous surface adsorption for FLX while CIP involved mainly heterogenous surface adsorption (Fig. 8b). Furthermore, the kinetics suggests an intraparticle diffusion (internal) as the major controlling step (Fig. 8b) and final interaction with active sites on the PET-FCQDs (Fig. 8c).

The adsorption mechanism was monitored by ATR-FTIR for before and after adsorption (Fig. 8a). For PET-FCQDs, characteristic peaks indicate the presence of specific functional groups. A broad band in the 3200–3500 cm-1 range corresponds to O–H stretching vibrations, suggesting hydroxyl groups on the surface. Peaks near 2900 cm-1 represent C–H stretching vibrations from aliphatic hydrocarbons. Strong peaks observed between 1700–1800 cm-1 are attributed to C = O stretching vibrations, likely from carboxyl or ester groups. Additionally, bands in the fingerprint region (500–1500 cm-1) signify C-O stretching, C–H bending, or aromatic ring vibrations. The spectrum of PET-FCQDs + FLX shows notable changes, reflecting their interactions. Shifts or intensity changes in the O–H and C = O stretching regions (3200–3500 cm-1 and 1700–1800 cm-1) suggest hydrogen bonding between FLX and the functional groups on the PET-FCQDs surface. New peaks or enhanced bands in the 1000–1500 cm-1 region may be linked to C-N stretching or aromatic ring vibrations from FLX. For PET-FCQDs + CIP, similar modifications are evident. Shifts in the O–H stretching region suggest hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl groups on the PET-FCQDs and CIP. Alterations in the fingerprint region include new peaks or increased intensities, potentially corresponding to C-F or C = O groups (around 1700 cm-1) from CIP. Enhanced or shifted peaks in the 1200–1600 cm-1 range highlight interactions involving the carboxyl and amine groups present in CIP. Comparing the spectra of PET-FCQDs, PET-FCQDs + FLX, and PET-FCQDs + CIP and the results from molecular dynamics confirms the successful adsorption of FLX and CIP.

Following molecular dynamics and ATR-FTIR, the adsorption mechanisms involved a combination of chemical and physical interactions, including electrostatic interactions, π–π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophobic effects (Fig. 8c), For FLX, adsorption occurs through several pathways. First, electrostatic interactions play a significant role as FLX, a weakly basic molecule, contains a secondary amine group that becomes protonated under neutral to slightly acidic conditions [26]. This positively charged FLX interacts with the negatively charged surface of PET-FCQDs, which possess C = O and O–H groups. Additionally, FLX contains aromatic rings that facilitate π–π stacking interactions with the graphitic structure of PET-FCQDs. Hydrogen bonding also contributes to the adsorption process, as the hydroxyl and amine groups in FLX interact with the oxygen-containing functional groups on the PET-FCQDs surface. Finally, hydrophobic interactions between the non-polar benzyl and trifluoromethyl groups of FLX and the hydrophobic regions of PET-FCQDs further stabilize the adsorption. For CIP, electrostatic interactions are important, as CIP is an amphoteric molecule with carboxyl and piperazine groups that vary in charge depending on the pH[20]. Under neutral to slightly basic conditions, CIP exists primarily as a zwitterion or anionic species, allowing it to interact strongly with the charged sites on PET-FCQDs. The quinolone ring in CIP enables π–π stacking interactions with the aromatic structure of PET-FCQDs, while its carboxyl and ketone groups form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the PET-FCQDs surface[21]. Hydrophobic effects also play a role, as the non-polar regions of CIP interact with the hydrophobic sections of PET-FCQDs. Generally, the adsorption of FLX and CIP onto PET-FCQDs is influenced by electrostatic interactions, π–π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophobic effects. The adsorption efficiency for both molecules is pH-dependent, as the ionization states of FLX, CIP, and PET-FCQDs change with pH. While FLX primarily relies on hydrophobic and aromatic π–π interactions, CIP benefits from a more diverse range of interactions, including its zwitterionic nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking analysis of PET-FCQDs as potential antidepressant and antibiotic
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 evaluates the antidepressant and antibiotic potential of PET-FCQDs in comparison with known drugs, FLX and CIP while the ligand–protein interactions is presented in Table S6. Docking scores and inhibition constants are used to assess interactions with key target proteins, specifically the serotonin transporter (SERT), DNA gyrase, and topoisomerase IV. Overall, PET-FCQDs appear to be promising multifunctional agents, but further experimental validation is needed to confirm these computational findings.

FLX is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) that works by inhibiting the serotonin transporter (SERT)[3]. By blocking SERT, FLX prevents the reabsorption of serotonin into presynaptic neurons, thereby increasing serotonin levels in the synaptic cleft and alleviating symptoms of depression[3]. In this study, FLX achieves a docking score of −8.4 kcal/mol and an inhibition constant of 6.91E-07 µM, indicating strong binding affinity to SERT. Interestingly, PET-FCQDs alone show an even better docking score of −8.7 kcal/mol and a lower inhibition constant of 4.16E-07 µM, suggesting they may have intrinsic antidepressant properties. However, the combination of PET-FCQDs with FLX results in a slightly weaker docking score of −8 kcal/mol and a higher inhibition constant of 1.34E-06 µM, potentially due to competitive or steric interference at the SERT binding site[28]. The FLX-SERT interaction analysis indicate (Table S6), FLX showed conventional hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions, particularly with residues such as Serine (SER) and isoleucine (ILE), which are important for its antidepressant activity[10,30]. PET-FCQDs alone displayed some weak binding interactions only with phenylalanine (PHE via π-π), alanine (ALA via π-alkyl) and glutamic acid (GLU via π-anion) with its aromatic rings, but when combined with FLX, interactions were changed with SER residues forming halogen bonds (Table S6). These combined interactions indicate that PET-FCQDs can potentially influence the binding efficacy of FLX through complementary effects, possibly reducing their biomedical applications.

CIP targets bacterial DNA replication by inhibiting DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV[12]. It disrupts these enzymes, preventing bacterial DNA replication and leading to cell death[12]. For DNA gyrase, CIP achieves a docking score of −7.4 kcal/mol with an inhibition constant of 3.74E-06 µM, demonstrating its effectiveness. PET-FCQDs alone show a stronger docking score of −7.8 kcal/mol and a lower inhibition constant of 1.90E-06 µM, indicating significant binding potential (Fig. 9). When adsorbed CIP (PET-FCQDs + CIP), the docking score remains the same (−7.4 kcal/mol), suggesting no significant effect. For topoisomerase IV (Fig. 9), CIP achieves a docking score of −7.5 kcal/mol and an inhibition constant of 3.16E-06 µM, reflecting strong binding. PET-FCQDs alone show weaker binding with a docking score of −7.2 kcal/mol and an inhibition constant of 5.24E-06 µM. The combination of PET-FCQDs and CIP slightly improves the docking score to −7.3 kcal/mol and reduces the inhibition constant to 4.43E-06 µM, though it remains less effective than CIP alone.

From the ligand–protein interactions analysis (Table S6), CIP exhibited strong binding with DNA gyrase, with conventional hydrogen bonds and charge interactions, particularly with tyrosine (TYR), arginine (ARG) and aspartic acid (ASP) (attractive charges) residues for its mechanism of action. PET-FCQDs, when interacting with DNA gyrase, showed similar but weaker interactions with ARG, ASP and asparagine (ASN) via π-, and van der Waal forces (TYR). PET-FCQDs + CIP displayed enhanced interaction profiles, combining hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions with introduction of new residues such as HIS via π-π. This suggests synergistic effects for PET-FCQDs binding efficacy modulated by CIP's. For Topoisomerase IV, when examining CIP alone, the interaction diagram shows significant binding with multiple amino acid residues including Proline (PRO), methionine (MET), GLU, and valine (VAL) residues, primarily stabilized through conventional hydrogen bonds, carbon-hydrogen bonds, and some unfavorable positive-positive interactions. The PET-FCQDs demonstrate a different binding pattern with fewer residue interactions, primarily engaging with GLU, VAL, and lysine (LYS) residues through Pi-cation and Pi-sigma interactions, suggesting substantial involvement of aromatic ring systems in the binding mechanism. The combined PET-FCQDs + CIP system exhibits a more complex interaction network involving histidine (HIS) and glycine (GLY), and PRO residues, featuring multiple interaction types including conventional hydrogen bonds, halogen (fluorine) interactions, and alkyl interactions. Critically, GLU emerges as a common interaction point across all three scenarios, suggesting its importance in the binding mechanism. The combination of PET-FCQDs and CIP appears to create a more diverse interaction profile compared to either component alone, indicating potential synergistic effects in their binding to Topoisomerase IV. This modified binding pattern in the presence of PET-FCQDs suggests possible implications for enhanced drug delivery efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion and toxicity (ADMET) of PET-FCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The success of a drug can be compromised by poor ADMET properties. Additionally, the high cost of clinical trials, largely due to pharmacokinetic challenges, remains a significant drawback in drug development[19]. The ADMET characteristics of PET-FCQDs were assessed using the SwissADME and ADMETSAR servers and presented in Table 3 . The ADMET analysis of PET-FCQDs highlights their potential as a promising drug candidate with favorable pharmacokinetic and safety profiles. PET-FCQDs exhibit moderate lipophilicity with Log P values ranging from 0.84 to 3.77 and good aqueous solubility (Log S = −5.02). High gastrointestinal (GI) absorption and acceptable skin permeability (Log Kp = -8.00 cm/s) indicate potential suitability for oral administration[5]. The compound complies with Lipinski’s Rule of Five and other drug-likeness criteria (Veber, Egan, Muegge) without any violations. Additionally, PET-FCQDs show no PAINS (pan assay interference compounds) alerts, suggesting specificity for intended targets, and have a synthetic accessibility score of 2.81, reflecting moderate ease of synthesis. However, Brenk alert due to the presence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), which are structural features that may indicate potential toxicity or undesirable drug-like properties[14]. PAHs are known for their stability and hydrophobic nature, which could affect bioavailability and interaction with biological systems[42]. In terms of leadlikeness, PET-FCQDs did not meet the criteria due to a violation of molecular weight (>350 Da). This suggests that PET-FCQDs might not align with the typical properties of lead compounds in drug discovery, which are optimized for oral bioavailability and pharmacokinetics.

Absorption studies predict good intestinal permeability (HIA + ) and blood–brain barrier penetration (BBB + ), enhancing its potential for central nervous system activity. PET-FCQDs are non-substrates and non-inhibitor for P-glycoprotein minimizing the risk of drug resistance due to efflux mechanisms[31,39]. Metabolism analysis shows they are non-substrates for key CYP450 enzymes (2C9, 2D6, 3A4), reducing the likelihood of metabolic interactions, with low CYP inhibitory promiscuity. In terms of toxicity, PET-FCQDs are classified as weakly toxic for acute oral intake (LD50 = 500–5000 mg/kg), non-carcinogenic, and non-ready for biodegradation. However, high toxicity to ecological organisms such fish, honey and Tetrahymena Pyriformis raises concerns about potential ecological risks if released into environments.

This study successfully demonstrated the efficient application of PET-FCQDs for the removal of FLX and CIP from aqueous solutions through an integrated approach combining experimental design, machine learning optimization, and molecular modeling. The Box-Behnken design effectively optimized the removal conditions, while machine learning models provided accurate predictions of removal efficiencies. The ANN model demonstrated superior predictive capability compared to SVM, achieving R2 values of 0.82 and 0.88 for FLX and CIP respectively. Under optimized conditions, exceptional removal rates of 95.19 % for FLX and 97.85 % for CIP were achieved, highlighting the effectiveness of the PET-FCQDs as an adsorbent. The adsorption process was best described by the Freundlich isotherm model (R2 > 0.998) and intraparticle diffusion kinetics (R2 > 0.94), indicating heterogeneous surface binding and diffusion-controlled adsorption. Molecular dynamics simulations and docking studies revealed multiple interaction mechanisms, including π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions, contributing to the high removal efficiencies. The FTIR and radial distribution function analyses further confirmed these binding mechanisms, showing significant molecular interactions between the pharmaceuticals and the PET-FCQDs surface. Molecular docking studies revealed strong binding affinities of PET-FCQDs to serotonin transporter (SERT), DNA gyrase, and topoisomerase IV, highlighting their intrinsic antidepressant and antibiotic potential. PET-FCQDs alone exhibited docking scores comparable to or exceeding those of FLX and CIP, suggesting they could play a complementary or even independent role in therapeutic applications. Adsorption of FLX and CIP on PET-FCQDs slightly modified their binding profiles, indicating synergistic or competitive interactions at the molecular level. In terms of toxicity, PET-FCQDs are classified as weakly toxic, non-carcinogenic, and non-ready for biodegradation. However, high toxicity to ecological organisms such fish, honey and Tetrahymena Pyriformis raises concerns about potential ecological risks if released into environments. The integration of experimental design with machine learning optimization proved to be a powerful approach for maximizing removal efficiencies while minimizing the number of experimental runs. The study showed that PET-FCQDs offer a dual role in environmental remediation and biomedical applications, showcasing versatility as both effective drug adsorbents and potential therapeutic agents. This study highlights the promising future of engineered carbon quantum dots in addressing global challenges in pollution and healthcare. Further investigations are warranted to explore their full potential in vivo and real-world applications.
This research was supported by the Japanese Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS KAKENHI Award number: 24KF0131) through a Special Research Fellowship application.
